heading: MODULUS SECUNDUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the second modulus of Zorzi's harmonic city with a "concentus" on the general division of the heavenly Jerusalem as described by Ezekiel and John the Evangelist, explaining why their accounts differ. Ezekiel divides the sacred structure into temple, city, and the promised region allotted to the twelve tribes with twelve gates, while John explicitly excludes the temple altogether ("Templum non vidi in ea"). Zorzi resolves the discrepancy typologically: Ezekiel describes the figurative household of the Old Covenant, which still required a physical temple for prayer and sacrifice, while John describes the Christian assembly, which is itself already built as God's temple according to Pauline doctrine. The chunk draws on John 4 (the dialogue with the Samaritan woman on worship in spirit and truth), John 14 (the promise of divine indwelling), and Revelation 21 (the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb as the temple), moving toward the eschatological vision of the river of living water and the tree of life.
essay:
## Overview and Structural Position

This brief but doctrinally dense passage inaugurates the *Modulus Secundus* of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*, specifically the first "concentus" (*Concentus I*) under the rubric *De generali partitione Ezechielis, et Iohannis* — the general partition of Ezekiel's and John's visions. The section is titled *Dispositio*, a term that signals the architectural-harmonic framework governing the entire *Tono Ottavo*: the "harmonic city" of the new Jerusalem is to be played, as Zorzi says, "tangentes secundum modulum nostrae harmonicae civitatis" ("touching [the strings] according to the measure of our harmonic city"). This musical-architectural metaphor is fundamental to Zorzi's project of reading Scripture through the lens of cosmic harmony.

## The Two Prophetic Visions and Their Divergence

Zorzi's central problem in this chunk is exegetical: Ezekiel (chapters 40–48) and John (Revelation 21–22) both describe an ideal sacred city, but their accounts appear to conflict on a crucial point. Ezekiel's vision articulates an elaborate, tripartite spatial hierarchy:

> Ezechiel enim prima partitione dividit fabricam in templum, civitatem, et totam promissam regionem, et hanc in duodecim partes, pro duodecim tribubus, cum duodecim portis. Sed et templum dividit in vestibula, thalamos, atria, gazophilatia, porticus, sancta sanctorum, et alias partes ad coquinam, et peculiares usus.

<cite>p. 2484</cite>

Ezekiel subdivides not only the regional territory into twelve tribal portions with twelve gates, but also breaks the temple itself into its constituent spaces: vestibules (*vestibula*), chambers (*thalami*), courts (*atria*), treasuries (*gazophilatia*), porticoes (*porticus*), the holy of holies (*sancta sanctorum*), and functional rooms for cooking and particular uses. This level of architectural granularity signals that Ezekiel is operating within the cultic logic of the Mosaic covenant, where a dedicated sacred precinct mediates between the holy and the profane.

John, by contrast, pointedly excludes the temple:

> Iohannes vero templum penitus excludit: Templum (inquit) non vidi in ea.

<cite>p. 2484</cite>

The Johannine city of Revelation 21:22 has no temple because, as the text continues, "the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple." Zorzi's harmonic reading must account for this structural absence.

## The Typological Resolution: Figurative Family vs. Christian Assembly

Zorzi resolves the apparent contradiction through a typological hermeneutic rooted in Pauline ecclesiology. Ezekiel describes the "figurativae familiae aedificia" — the buildings of the figurative household — which, operating under the Old Covenant's logic, necessarily included a physical temple for prayer and sacrifice. John, writing for the Christian community, describes instead the "domus et civitatem exarat Christiani coetus" — the house and city of the Christian assembly — whose members need no external temple because they themselves have already been built as God's temple.

The Pauline subtext is 1 Corinthians 3:16 / 2 Corinthians 6:16 (the believer as temple of the Holy Spirit), but Zorzi also invokes two key Johannine passages. First, the dialogue with the Samaritan woman (John 4:21–23):

> Veniet tempus, et nunc est, quando veri adoratores adorabunt Patrem non in monte hoc, aut templo, sed in spiritu, et veritate.

<cite>p. 2484</cite>

This verse is pivotal for Zorzi's argument: Christ's own words abolish the geographical-architectonic specificity of worship (neither Gerizim nor Jerusalem), relocating the locus of true adoration to an interior, spiritual space. The Christian, purified in via, becomes the dwelling-place of God through "pietatem, mundiciam, et bona opera" (piety, purity, and good works).

## Divine Indwelling and Eschatological Citizenship

The second Johannine text is John 14:23, the promise of the Father and Son taking up residence in the obedient believer:

> Si quis sermonem meum servaverit, Pater meus diliget eum, et ad eum veniemus, et mansionem apud eum faciemus.

<cite>p. 2484–2485</cite>

This promise grounds the present tense of divine indwelling: already here below, the believer "domestice suscipimus Deum hospitem" (receives God as a guest within the household). The eschatological intensification then follows: in the eternal tabernacles, the relationship will be reversed — we will be received by God, seeing him "aperto contuitu, facie ad faciem, et mutua consuetudine" (with open gaze, face to face, and in mutual fellowship), as citizens of the heavenly city.

The final citation returns to Revelation 21:22: "Dominus enim omnipotens, templum illius est, et agnus" — the Lord Almighty and the Lamb are themselves the temple of that city, the assembly of the blessed ascending to it. The open gates through which all may enter, the luminous splendor illuminating all, the river of living water, and the tree of life in the midst of the piazza are then mentioned as further features John describes, though the text breaks off at the page boundary before developing them.

## Significance Within Zorzi's Harmonic Project

This concentus performs essential theoretical work for the *Modulus Secundus*. By establishing that the Christian assembly is itself the living temple — a position simultaneously Pauline, Johannine, and Neoplatonic in its internalization of sacred space — Zorzi prepares the ground for reading the architecture of the new Jerusalem not as a literal future building programme but as a harmonic map of spiritual ascent. The twelve gates, the splendor, the river of living water will be treated in subsequent concentus as harmonically organized thresholds and flows in the soul's journey toward the beatific vision. The typological contrast between Ezekiel (figure) and John (fulfillment) also allows Zorzi to incorporate the full weight of Ezekiel's extraordinarily detailed architectural vision while ultimately transcending it through the Johannine spiritualization — a characteristic move of his synthetic Christian Kabbalist hermeneutics.
